# <span style="color: #1a3a52">IELTS Listening Master Guide</span>

---

#ielts #listening #band9



> [!info] **Band 9 Rule**
> Band 9 targets normally require near-perfect control in Parts 1 and 2, then strong tactical control in Parts 3 and 4.


![[Pasted image 20260424214822.png]]

### Listen once rule
> [!warning] Band Penalty
> On exam day, the recording plays once only. Do not train by replaying audio multiple times to find answers.

## <span style="color: #00838f">Test Sections Overview</span>

### <span style="color: #1b5e20">Section 1: Everyday Social Context (Conversation)</span>

- **Format:** A phone call or face-to-face conversation between two people (e.g., booking a transport ticket, inquiring about shipping).
- **Pacing & Style:** Slow and clear. Usually involves Form Filling (names, numbers, addresses).
- **Breaks:** There is a short break in the middle of the section (e.g., "Look at questions 1 to 6").
-  **The Challenge:** This is a highly unnatural way to listen to English. We rarely listen to people spelling out random alphanumeric sequences in daily life.
- **Band 9 Strategy:**
    - [ ] Do not rely on podcasts for this.
    - [ ] Use **only official Cambridge listening tests** to drill Part 1 until form-filling feels completely automatic.
![[Pasted image 20260424192335.png]]
> [!tip] 
> Keep your headphone volume slightly higher here. The "receiver's" voice on the simulated phone call is intentionally made to sound fainter.

### <span style="color: #1b5e20">Section 2: Everyday Social Context (Monologue)</span>

- **Format:** One person speaking about a non-academic topic (e.g., introducing a theme park, explaining a community center's layout, radio broadcast).
- **Breaks:** Features a mid-section break.
- **Common Types:** Map labeling, short Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs).

* **Band 9 Strategy:**
    - [ ] Listen to solo-hosted podcasts or single-speaker YouTube videos.
    - [ ] **Do not listen to IELTS material for this.** IELTS audio can be boring. Pick a topic you actually love and listen to it on the bus or during lunch to naturally build single-speaker comprehension.
### <span style="color: #1b5e20">Section 3: Academic Context (Conversation)</span>

- **Format:** A discussion involving 2 to 4 people (e.g., two students and a professor discussing a project or research).
- **Difficulty:** Often considered the trickiest due to lengthy MCQs and heavy conversational distractions (speakers changing their minds or debating).
- **Breaks:** Features a mid-section break.
- **The Challenge:** Differentiating between multiple voices, accents, and overlapping thoughts.
- **Band 9 Strategy:**
    - [ ] Listen to panel podcasts or YouTube debates on mildly academic subjects (history, science, health).
    - [ ] Focus on *how* people interject, signal agreement/disagreement, and express nuanced opinions.
### <span style="color: #1b5e20">Section 4: Academic Context (Monologue)</span>

- **Format:** A university lecture or expert presentation on an academic subject (e.g., biology, history, engineering).
- **Format Trap:** **There are no mid-section breaks.** You must read and track questions 31-40 continuously for about 7-8 minutes.
- **Common Types:** Summary or Note Completion.
- **Band 9 Strategy:**
    - [ ] Consume TED Talks (via ted.com or YouTube).
    - [ ] Listen for **Staging Language** (e.g., "Firstly...", "Now we will move on to..."). This language is the map that prevents you from getting lost in the audio.

---

## <span style="color: #d4af37">Core Challenges: Accents, Alphabets, & Numbers</span>

### <span style="color: #1b5e20">The British Accent</span>

- Expect 85-90% of the audio to feature British accents (as Cambridge creates the test).
- **Actionable Advice:**
    - [ ] Watch British movies, series (like Harry Potter or Sherlock), and BBC documentaries to tune your ear to the phrasing and intonation.

### <span style="color: #d4af37">Alphabet Traps</span>

- **A vs. I:** This is the most common pitfall for Asian/non-native speakers during spelling dictations.
    - In a British accent, the letter **"A"** is pronounced similarly to how non-natives might expect **"I"** to sound (with a drawn-out 'ay' drop).
    - Pay intense attention when names (e.g., M-A-T-T-H-E-W) or postcodes are dictated.

### <span style="color: #d4af37">Number Traps</span>

- **The Zero (0):** Rarely pronounced as "zero". Listen for **"Nought"**, **"Null"**, or the letter **"Oh"** (e.g., 404 is often said as "Four-oh-four").
- **The "Mini-Pause" Technique:** Speakers dictate long numbers (phone numbers, IDs) in clusters. They do not say them at a flat rhythm.
    - *Example:* "635 [pause] 623 [pause] 6295".
    - *Strategy:* Wait for the mini-pause before writing the cluster down to avoid panicking and falling behind.

### <span style="color: #5e35b1">The "Verb / W-Method" for Remembering Questions</span>

> [!tip] **Memory Hack**
> Turning pages between the questions and the passage/audio wastes critical time. You must learn to remember the core of the question.
> - **Find the Verb:** Look for the main action verb in the sentence.
> - **Create a W-Question:** Frame it in your mind using Who, What, When, Where.
> - *Example Question:* "Mary became interested in science when she was a child."
> - *Mental Anchor:* "Who became interested in what, and when?" This anchors the concept in your memory so you don't have to keep re-reading the question booklet.



---


> [!warning] **The Flaw of Normal Studying**
> The human brain is not naturally wired to focus intensely on foreign audio for 30 consecutive minutes. If your first attempt at 30 minutes of deep focus is on Test Day, you will fail.

### Build Stamina Like a Marathon Runner

- **The Analogy:** You do not run a 25-mile marathon on day one. You run 5 miles, then 8, then 12.
- **The Execution:**
    - [ ] Set a timer. Listen with 100% focus for 5 minutes. The next day, do 7 minutes. Then 10, 12, and 15. Treat your attention span as a physical muscle that must be progressively overloaded.

### The "Spoiled Child" Syndrome (Listen ONLY Once)

- **The Reality:** Real life does not have a rewind button. University lecturers and airport announcers will not repeat themselves five times.
- **The Mistake:** English teachers "spoil" their students by playing tracks 3 or 4 times to be liked. This destroys your test readiness.
- **The Execution:**
    - [ ] When practicing, force yourself to listen to audio *exactly once*. Think, answer, and only check the transcript afterward.

### Radical Focus & Distraction Detox

- **Meditation:** Practice 5–10 minutes of meditation between study blocks to clear the cognitive load and reset your brain.
- **The Information Diet:** Band 9 students actively eliminate distractions leading up to the test:
    - [x] Delete social media apps from your phone (use blockers). ✅ 2026-04-27
    - [x] Stop watching the news entirely (you do not need global anxieties draining your mental battery). ✅ 2026-04-27
    - [ ] Temporarily distance yourself from "draining people" who make their personal problems your problems while you are studying.

<br>
## <span style="color: #5e35b1">Technical Execution on Test Day</span>

### <span style="color: #1b5e20">The Computer-Based Advantage</span>
Whenever geographically possible, book the computer-delivered test.

- [ ] **Acoustic Isolation:** You are guaranteed personal headphones, which dramatically block out ambient noise.
- [ ] **Physical Ease:** Less handwriting, zero page-flipping. You just click multiple-choice options or type directly.
- [ ] **Low Stress Environment:** Test rooms hold 7–8 people instead of massive halls with 50–100 stressed candidates.
- [ ] *Pro-Tip for Paper Tests:* If you must do paper, **complain on test day** if the audio is bad or the headphones are broken. Emailing the British Council the next day accomplishes absolutely nothing. Use a pencil so you can erase quickly without panicking.

### <span style="color: #5e35b1">Capital Letters</span>

- Every decision you make drains your brain's "battery." 
- Do not waste energy wondering if "airport" needs a capital A. 
- **The Rule:** ==Write absolutely everything in ALL CAPS.==

### <span style="color: #00838f">The Exact Mathematics of Word Counts</span>

> [!info] **Word Count Rule**
> Read instructions surgically. Articles and prepositions count as full words.
> - *Instruction:* "Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER"
>     - `Terminal 2` = Correct (1 word, 1 number)
>     - `2` = Correct (1 number)
>     - `Heathrow Terminal 2` = Correct (2 words, 1 number)
>     - `in Terminal 2` = INCORRECT (2 words, 1 number -> Wait, "in Terminal" is 2 words, so "in Terminal 2" is valid under "two words AND a number". However, writing `Heathrow Airport Terminal 2` is 3 words and a number, which is a hard fail).
> - *Hyphen Rule:* `ex-examiner` counts as strictly **one** word.

### <span style="color: #1b5e20">Peripheral Listening & The "Murder Hole"</span>

- **Peripheral Listening (The Driving Analogy):** When driving, you look straight ahead, but you are peripherally aware of cars to the side and behind you. In IELTS, focus on the current question, but keep the *next* heading or keyword in your peripheral vision so you know when the topic shifts.
- **Keep Moving (The SAS Analogy):** British Special Forces call the doorway of a room the "murder hole." If you freeze in the doorway, you die. If you miss a question on the IELTS, do not freeze in the murder hole. Guess immediately and move to the next question. Panic causes a domino effect of missed answers.



## <span style="color: #d4af37">Decrypting IELTS Traps and Distractors</span>

*Cambridge is not trying to "trick" you maliciously; they are simulating real-world miscommunications (e.g., a doctor asking for 25cc of adrenaline, then correcting to 50cc).*

> [!danger] **The 5 Classic Traps**
> 1. **The 'Changed Mind':** "We started in London... actually, no, we arrived in Manchester." If you write the first place you hear, you fail.
> 2. **Paraphrasing/Synonyms:** Question says `destination`. Audio says `coming back to`. You must listen for *meaning*, not exact words.
> 3. **Plurals vs. Singular:** Question context: "Do you take sugar?" Audio: "I take two sugar**s**." Missing the tiny 's' or 'z' sound makes the answer 100% incorrect.
> 4. **The Unusual Spelling:** Common names with weird spellings (e.g., `Browne` instead of `Brown`, or `Pell` instead of `Bell`). If they spell it out letter-by-letter in Part 1, ignore your instinct and write *exactly* the letters spoken.
> 5. **The Double Negative:** Audio: "The food wasn't bad, but the drinks were terrible." Question: *What were they least satisfied with?* Answer: Drinks. ("Wasn't bad" = Good).

<br>


## <span style="color: #5e35b1">The "Perfect Practice" Framework</span>

> [!danger] **The Fake Test Epidemic**
> **90% of IELTS tests on YouTube and random blogs are fake.** They are created for views, not accuracy. They are either too easy (giving false confidence) or too hard (destroying morale). 
> **ONLY USE:** IDP, British Council, IELTS.org, or official Cambridge English books.

### <span style="color: #1b5e20">The 3-Step Remediation Loop</span>
Practicing mindlessly does not increase your score. You must diagnose the disease.


<summary>Show Remediation Loop</summary>

1.  **The Exam Condition Benchmark:**
    - [ ] Take an official test under strict time limits with zero cheating.
    - [ ] Do this three times. If you are not consistently hitting 0.5 to 1.0 bands *above* your target score, **stop taking practice tests**.
2.  **The Autopsy (Checking the Audio Script):**
    - [ ] Go to the transcript at the back of the book. Find exactly *why* you failed a question.
    - [ ] *Was it Spelling?* → Add it to your digital "Spelling Mistakes" `.doc`. Test yourself on this document weekly.
    - [ ] *Was it a specific Question Type (e.g., Maps)?* → You don't lack English skills; you lack tactical strategy. Rewatch the map strategy section.
    - [ ] *Was it Focus?* → Increase your meditation and focus-stamina training.
    - [ ] *Was it Vocabulary?* → If you stared at the transcript and still didn't know what the words meant, more practice tests will not save you. You must stop testing and start a dedicated vocabulary expansion routine.
3.  **Targeted Healing:**
    - [ ] Isolate the specific weakness found in Step 2. Do focused drills exclusively on that weakness until it becomes a strength. Only then do you return to full practice tests. 
    - [ ] On Test Day, the process should feel as automatic and stress-free as tying your shoelaces.


## <span style="color: #5e35b1">Detailed Question Types & Specific Strategies</span>

### <span style="color: #1b5e20">Form Completion</span>

- **Instructions Check:** Strictly adhere to the word count constraint (e.g., *"No more than two words and/or a number"").
    - "Two words and a number" means `9:30 a.m.` (1 number, 1 word) is valid. `Two little birds` (2 words, 1 number) is valid.

- **Units Rule:** Look at the blank. If the unit is already printed (e.g., `____ meters` or `$ ____`), **do not** write the unit in your answer. If the unit is missing, you must include it (e.g., write `8 kg`, not just `8`).

- **Speed Writing:** Jot down answers quickly and messily in your question booklet so you don't lose the audio flow. Handwriting only matters on the final transfer sheet.
![[Pasted image 20260424194059.png]]
- **Rows and Columns:** Do not just look at the blanks. The column headers and row titles are massive hints.

### <span style="color: #1b5e20">Table Completion</span>

- **Rows and Columns:** Do not just look at the blanks. The column headers and row titles are massive hints. 
![[Pasted image 20260424203619.png]]

- **Prediction:** If a column header says "Preferred Climate" and the row above says "Temperate", you can logically predict the blank below will be a weather/climate word (e.g., "Subtropical").

- **Sequence:** The audio will strictly follow the numbering of the blanks.
![[Pasted image 20260424201712.png]]
![[Pasted image 20260424205226.png]]

### <span style="color: #1b5e20">Sentence Completion</span>

- **Expect Paraphrasing:** You will *never* hear the exact sentence printed on your paper. The audio will use synonyms and flipped sentence structures.
- **Exact Words Required:** While the prompt is paraphrased, your answer must be the **exact word(s) spoken in the audio**. Do not write your own synonyms.
- **Grammar Check:** When you fill in the blank, read the sentence silently. If it is grammatically incorrect, you likely wrote down the wrong word.

![[Pasted image 20260424205931.png]]


### <span style="color: #1b5e20">Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs)</span>

- **The Core Problem:** Reading lengthy options while listening to complex audio is nearly impossible.
- **Prep-Time Strategy:** During the 30 seconds of prep time, **do not read the options (A, B, C)**. Only read the Questions/Statements. 
- **Concise the Options:** If you must read options, cross out filler words. Condense long options into 2-3 keywords. 
- ![[Pasted image 20260425095126.png]]
- **Prep-Time Strategy:** During the 30 seconds of prep time, **do not read the options (A, B, C)**. Only read the Questions/Statements.
- **Concise the Options:** If you must read options, cross out filler words. Condense long options into 2-3 keywords.
- **Post-Test Analysis:** When practicing, always ask: *"Why did I get it right?"* and *"Why did I fall for the wrong answer/distraction?"* This builds immunity to IELTS traps.
![[Pasted image 20260424215250.png]]
![[Pasted image 20260424215117.png]]
*This is not a listening test. It is a listening AND thinking test.*
1.  **Read Immediately:** Do not check your previous answers during pauses. Use every second to read the upcoming MCQs.
2.  **Keyword Isolation:** Underline the core concept in the question stem.
3.  **Find the Delta:** Analyze the exact difference between options A, B, and C. (e.g., If A is "Good value for money" and B is "More useful than other appliances," note that one is financial and one is comparative).
4.  **Listen to the WHOLE Section:** Do not tick the first match you hear. Listen to the speaker's complete thought, as they will often use a qualifier like *but* or *however* at the very end of the sentence to flip the meaning.
5.  **Educated Elimination:** If lost, cross out the objectively wrong answer to increase your guessing odds from 33% to 50%. Pick one and move on immediately.


### <span style="color: #1b5e20">Short Answer Questions</span>

- **Identify the 'Wh' Word:** Focus entirely on whether the question asks *What*, *Which*, *Where*, or *Who*.
- **Stay Ahead:** Read one question ahead of the audio. As soon as you write the answer to Q11, your eyes should immediately be locked onto Q12.
![[Pasted image 20260425100719.png]]


### <span style="color: #1b5e20">Map Labeling</span>
- **Type 1 (Simple):** The questions are inside the map. The audio will sequentially guide you from Question 11 to 12 to 13.
    
- **Type 2 (Difficult):** The options (A, B, C...) are on the map, and the questions are outside. You have to locate the matching letter for the spoken location.
    
- **The TMI Strategy (Temporary Memory Invocation):**
    
    - The biggest problem with maps is trying to read the questions, listen to the audio, and look at the map simultaneously.
        
    - **Solution:** Spend 9–10 seconds intensely staring at the map before the audio starts to burn the layout into your temporary memory.
        
    - Locate the entry point (e.g., "North Gate").
        
    - Understand the landmarks (e.g., "Library is left of the desk", "North is up, South is down, West is left, East is right").
        
    - By memorizing the layout, you won't have to rapidly dart your eyes between the paper and the map, reducing mistakes.
![[Pasted image 20260425103258.png]]
![[Pasted image 20260425104229.png]]
![[Pasted image 20260425104319.png]]

<summary>Show Map & Diagram Labeling Tips</summary>

*The core issue here is "visualization" and spatial vocabulary.*
1.  **Vocab Prep:** Master terms like *adjacent to*, *to the east*, *bend in the road*.
2.  **Analyze the Board:** Find the absolute starting point (e.g., North Gate).
3.  **Mental Walkthrough:** Before the audio starts, predict the routes. *"If I go through the South Gate, the lake is on my left and the woods are on my right."*
4.  **Synonym Hunting:** If a label says "Cafe", think *coffee shop* or *restaurant*. If it says "Toilets", think *bathroom* or *facilities*.
5.  **Listen for the Flags:** Track signposting language ("Let's start off at...", "Moving past the...").
6.  **Beware the "Ghost Building":** They will often describe a building that *used* to be there, or is *planned* for the future, to trick you. Listen for present-tense reality.




### <span style="color: #00838f">Advanced Spatial & Map Vocabulary (Expansion for Map Labeling)</span>
.

●     **Location – where something is in relation to another object or place.**

●     **Direction – the position towards which someone moves or faces.**

Common vocabulary of location:

**near, in front of, next to, beside, across from, between**

Common vocabulary of direction:

**turn right, turn left, go straight on, travel northwest, go past**

Below, you’ll find lists of vocabulary commonly used in map and plan questions, along with sample sentences to show the words and phrases used in context. Each set of sentences describes or gives directions on a map or plan to give you some practice at visualising the information as you'll have to do in your test.

We’ll start with some simple prepositions of place.

---

#### **IELTS Listening Map Vocabulary – Set 1**

####  

●     **facing**

●     **in the middle / centre of**

●     **behind**

●     **between**

●     **next to / by**

●     **in front of**

**![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)**

                              Source: Cambridge English past paper

The entrance is **in front of** the reception desk.

The reception desk is **facing** the entrance.

The pool is **in the centre of** / **in the middle of** the sport’s centre.

The gym is **behind** the swimming pool.

The pool is **between** the reception and the gym.

The seating area is **next to** / **by** the pool.

---

#### **IELTS Listening Map Vocabulary – Set 2**

####  

●     **left-hand side**

●     **right-hand side**

●     **turn right**

●     **to your right**

●     **pass through**

●     **on the other side**

●     **beyond**

●     **along**

●     **corner**

●     **go straight**

●     **beside**

![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)

                              Source: Cambridge English past paper

The sports shop is on the **left-hand side** as you enter the sports centre.

To the **right-hand side** of the entrance, there’s a café.

**Pass through** the entrance, **turn right** and the café is immediately **in front of** you.

The leisure pool is **on the other side** of the sports centre to the dance studios.

There is a large changing room just **beyond** the café.

Walk **along** the side of the sports hall and you’ll find the second dance studio in the far **corner** of the building

**Go straight** along the corridor **beside** the sports hall and **to your right**, you’ll see two dance studios.

---

#### **IELTS Listening Map Vocabulary – Set 3**



Maps nearly always have compass points shown. You need to know the 8 points of the compass as these are often used in the recording to describe position. The 8 points are:

●     **north**

●     **south**

●     **east**

●     **west**

●     **northeast**

●     **northwest**

●     **southeast**

●     **southwest**

There are several different ways you might hear them used.

●     **to the north / to the south …**

●     **in the northeast / in the southwest …**

●     **north side / east side / west side / south side**

●     **heading east**

●     **slightly west of**

●     **just southeast of**

Other vocabulary:

●     **near**

●     **first / second turning**

●     **on the left**

●     **straight ahead**

**![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)**

                              Source: Cambridge English past paper

The houses immediately **to the north** and **south** of the Post Office are very **near** the river.

The bridge is just beyond the final row of houses **heading north**.

After crossing the bridge, take the **second turning** **on the left** and you’ll see the retirement home **straight ahead**.

The retirement home is **just southeast** of the primary school.

The post office is **to the northwest** of the retirement home.

The village of Stokeford is **slightly east** of the River Stoke.

There is a housing estate to the **west side** of the retirement home.

---

#### **IELTS Listening Map Vocabulary – Set 4**



●     **opposite**

●     **across the road**

●     **same side**

●     **before you get to**

●     **junction**

●     **dead end**

●     **leads off**

**![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)**

The chemist is **opposite** a supermarket.

They are going to build new houses **across the road** from the school at point G.

There is a supermarket on the **same side** of the road as the library.

Walk west along High Street and you’ll pass the library **before you get to** the **junction** with Station Road.

The bank is on a **dead end** road that **leads off** the High Street.

---

#### **IELTS Listening Map Vocabulary – Set 5**

####  

●     **bend**

●     **just past**

●     **alongside**

●     **adjoining**

●     **carry straight on**

**![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)**

                              Source: Cambridge English past paper

The school is situated on a **bend** in the dual carriageway.

The bus station is on the right **just past** the shopping centre.

The town centre has a pedestrian walkway running **alongside** a row of **adjoining** shops.

After passing the school on your right, **carry straight on** and you’ll see the park on your left around the next bend.

#### Advanced Map Directions & Layouts



![[Pasted image 20260428010539.png]]
![[Pasted image 20260428010644.png]]
---

### 7.7 Flowchart Completion

- **The Title:** The heading of the flowchart gives you the context of the entire process (e.g., "How a caloric restriction mimetic works").
- **Follow the Arrows:** The sequence is absolute. Follow the flow strictly.
- **Track Empty Boxes:** Even if a box doesn't have a question in it, listen to the audio describe it so you know exactly when the speaker is transitioning to the box that *does* have your question. 
- **Grammar Constraints:** Flowcharts use short phrases. Predict if the blank requires a noun, a verb, or an adjective based on the words immediately preceding it.
![[Pasted image 20260425105001.png]]


### 7.8 Matching Features

- **The Setup:** Matching a list of options (features/names/dates) to a list of numbered questions.
- **Check for Repetition:** Look at the instructions. If it says *"<mark style="background: #ABF7F7A6;">You may use any letter more than once</mark>,"* expect at least one option to be the answer to multiple questions. If it doesn't say this, use process of elimination.
- **Listen for Consensus:** In Section 3, Speaker A might suggest matching item X with option B. Speaker B might disagree and suggest option C. Speaker A will then agree to C. The correct answer is C. You must wait for the conclusion of the dialogue.
- **Probability Words:** Focus heavily on modal verbs and certainty markers (*will definitely, might, probably won't*). These dictate the final match.

![[Pasted image 20260425123405.png]]


---

## 8. <mark style="background: #CACFD9A6;">Spelling Mastery</mark>


### 8.1 Comprehensive Synonyms by Category

#### 8.1.1 Employment & Business

| Target Word    | Synonyms/Paraphrases                                          |        |
| :------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------ | ------ |
| ==Profession== | ==Career, Occupation, Job, Vocation, Field of work==          | PCOJVF |
| ==Staff==      | ==Employees, Personnel, Workforce, Team members, Associates== |        |
| ==Manager==    | ==Supervisor, Boss, Head of Department, Executive, Director== |        |
| ==Deadline==   | ==Due date, Time limit, Cut-off point, Target date==          |        |
| ==Recruit==    | ==Hire, Employ, Take on, Enlist==                             |        |
| ==Salary==     | ==Wages, Income, Earnings, Pay, Remuneration==                |        |
| ==Dismiss==    | ==Fire, Let go, Make redundant, Terminate==                   | DLMT   |

#### 8.1.2 Environment & Science

| Target Word    | Synonyms/Paraphrases                                   |
| :------------- | :----------------------------------------------------- |
| ==Environment==    | ==Habitat, Ecosystem, Surroundings, Biosphere==            |
| ==Pollution==      | ==Contamination, Toxic waste, Emissions, Pollutants==      |
| ==Conserve==       | ==Preserve, Protect, Save, Safeguard, Maintain==           |
| ==Global Warming== | ==Climate change, Rising temperatures, Greenhouse effect== |
| ==Agriculture==    | ==Farming, Cultivation, Crop production, Agronomy==        |
| ==Disaster==       | ==Catastrophe, Calamity, Emergency, Tragedy==              |
| ==Species==        | ==Organisms, Creatures, Flora and fauna==                  |

#### 8.1.3 Scale, Time, and Quantity

| Target Word | Synonyms/Paraphrases                                   |
| :---------- | :----------------------------------------------------- |
| ==Enormous==    | ==Massive, Huge, Vast, Significant, Substantial, Immense== |
| ==Tiny==        | ==Minuscule, Microscopic, Small, Minute, Negligible==      |
| ==Annual==      | ==Yearly, Once a year, Every twelve months==               |
| ==Decade==      | ==Ten years, A 10-year period==                            |
| ==Century==     | ==One hundred years, A 100-year span==                     |
| ==Fortnight==   | ==Two weeks, Fourteen days==                               |
| ==Majority==    | ==Most of, The bulk of, The greater part of==              |

#### 8.1.4 Maps, Directions & Locations

| Target Word/Phrase | Synonyms/Paraphrases                                   |
| :----------------- | :----------------------------------------------------- |
| ==Next to==            | ==Adjacent to, Alongside, Adjoining, By, Flanking==        |
| ==Opposite==           | ==Facing, Across from, On the other side of==              |
| ==In the middle==      | ==In the center, Centrally located, Halfway, Amidst==      |
| ==At the top/bottom==  | ==To the north/south, At the upper/lower end==             |
| ==Intersection==       | ==Junction, Crossroads, Where the paths meet, Roundabout== |
| ==Path==               | ==Trail, Track, Footpath, Walkway, Route==                 |
| ==Go straight==        | ==Head right forward, Continue ahead, Proceed directly==   |
| ==Station==            | ==Destination, Terminus, Stop==                            |
| ==Returning to==       | ==Return, Come back, Final destination, Head back==        |
| ==Cafe==               | ==Coffee shop, Restaurant, Cafeteria, Bistro, Eatery==     |
| ==Toilets==            | ==Washroom, Bathroom, Restrooms, Lavatory, Facilities==    |
| ==Woods==              | ==Forest, Trees, Copse, Woodland, Thicket==                |

| :---------------- | :---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --- |

| ==Theory==        | ==Hypothesis, Premise, Supposition, Concept==                                       |     |

| Show              | Demonstrate, Illustrate, Exhibit, Reveal, Display, Depict, Portray                  |     |
| Make              | Create, Generate, Produce, Construct, Devise, Forge, Formulate                      |     |

| Say               | State, Declare, Assert, Announce, Mention, Remark, Articulate                       |     |

| Decrease          | Diminish, Reduce, Lessen, Curtail, Lower, Minimize, Shrink                          |     |

| Result       | Outcome, Consequence, Ramification, Aftermath, Effect, Repercussion                 | ROCRAER       |


### 8.2 <mark style="background: #CACFD9A6;">British vs. American, Double Consonants, and Silent Letters</mark>


#### 8.2.1 British vs. American Spelling
* -our vs -or: Colour, Flavour, Humour, Labour, Neighbour (British)
* -ise vs -ize: Organise, Recognise, Realise, Prioritise (British preferred)
* -re vs -er: Centre, Theatre, Metre, Litre, Fibre (British)
* -ce vs -se: Defence, Offence, Pretence (British)
* Double 'L' in suffixes: Travelled, Travelling, Cancelled, Fuelling (British)

#### 8.2.2 Double Consonant Traps

Accommodation (Two C's, Two M's)
Assessment (Four S's total)
Committee (Two M's, Two T's, Two E's)
Disappoint (One S, Two P's)
Embarrass (Two R's, Two S's)
Environment (Don't forget the 'N' before the 'M')
Government (Don't forget the 'N')
Guarantee (U before A)
Millennium (Two L's, Two N's)
Necessary (One C, Two S's)
Occurrence (Two C's, Two R's)
Professor (One F, Two S's)
Questionnaire (Two N's)
Recommend (One C, Two M's)
Successful (Two C's, Two S's)

#### 8.2.3 Silent Letters & Homophones

### 9. <mark style="background: #CACFD9A6;">Synonyms, Grammar, and Prediction Mastery</mark>

#### 9.1 Paraphrasing Skills for IELTS Listening

> [!info] **Why Paraphrasing Matters**
> IELTS Listening rarely uses the exact words from the question. Instead, the audio paraphrases, rewords, or uses synonyms. Band 9 scorers train to instantly recognize meaning, not just match words.

##### 9.1.1 What is Paraphrasing?
Paraphrasing is expressing the same idea in a different way. In IELTS Listening, this means:
- Using synonyms (e.g., 'purchase' for 'buy')
- Changing word forms (e.g., 'decision' for 'decide')
- Flipping sentence structure (e.g., 'The meeting was cancelled' → 'They decided not to hold the meeting')
- Using definitions or explanations instead of the exact word

##### 9.1.2 Core Paraphrasing Strategies
1. **Synonym Substitution**
    - Audio: "purchase a ticket" → Question: "buy a ticket"
    - Audio: "assist customers" → Question: "help clients"
2. **Word Class Change**
    - Audio: "the construction of the bridge" → Question: "the bridge was constructed"
    - Audio: "improvement in health" → Question: "health improved"
3. **Passive/Active Voice Change**
    - Audio: "The company was founded in 1990" → Question: "They founded the company in 1990"
4. **Definition or Explanation**
    - Audio: "She is bilingual" → Question: "She speaks two languages"
    - Audio: "The event was postponed" → Question: "The event will happen later than planned"
5. **Negative/Positive Flip**
    - Audio: "He failed to arrive on time" → Question: "He was late"
    - Audio: "Not allowed" → Question: "Prohibited"
6. **Collocation and Fixed Phrase Substitution**
    - Audio: "catch a cold" → Question: "become ill"
    - Audio: "make a decision" → Question: "decide"

##### 9.1.3 How to Train Paraphrasing for IELTS Listening
1. **Practice with Official Tests:**
    - After each test, underline all words in the questions that were paraphrased in the audio. Make a list of these pairs.
2. **Build a Synonym Bank:**
    - For common IELTS topics (education, health, environment, travel, technology), list key words and their synonyms.
3. **Listen for Meaning, Not Words:**
    - When practicing, focus on the meaning of the sentence, not just the words used.
4. **Shadowing and Rewording:**
    - Listen to a sentence, pause, and try to rephrase it in your own words.
5. **Use Paraphrasing Apps or Tools:**
    - Tools like Thesaurus.com, Ludwig.guru, or even Google can help you find alternative ways to say things.

##### 9.1.4 Paraphrasing Practice Table
| Question Word/Phrase | Audio Paraphrase         | Topic         |
| :------------------ | :----------------------- | :------------ |
| children            | young people, kids       | Education     |
| job                 | occupation, profession   | Work          |
| environment         | surroundings, habitat    | Environment   |
| illness             | disease, sickness        | Health        |
| journey             | trip, travel             | Travel        |
| advantage           | benefit, positive aspect | General       |
| disadvantage        | drawback, negative aspect| General       |
| increase            | rise, go up, grow        | Data/Trends   |
| decrease            | fall, go down, drop      | Data/Trends   |
| expensive           | costly, high-priced      | Shopping      |
| cheap               | low-cost, inexpensive    | Shopping      |
| improve             | enhance, boost           | General       |
| start               | begin, commence, launch  | General       |
| finish              | end, complete, conclude  | General       |
| main                | primary, chief, principal| General       |
| difficult           | hard, challenging        | General       |
| easy                | simple, straightforward  | General       |

##### 9.1.5 Advanced Paraphrasing Tips for Band 9
- **Expect multi-step paraphrasing:** Sometimes, the audio will paraphrase both the subject and the verb (e.g., 'students' → 'young people', 'attend' → 'go to').
- **Watch for distractors:** The audio may use a synonym for a wrong answer before giving the correct one. Listen to the whole sentence.
- **Practice with transcripts:** After listening, compare the transcript to the questions and highlight all paraphrased sections.

> [!tip] **Band 9 Habit:**
> After every practice test, make a "Paraphrase Journal"—a list of all paraphrased words/phrases you missed. Review it before your next test.


#### 9.2 Expanded Singular/Plural/Irregular Noun Table
| Singular | Plural | Irregular/Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Analysis | Analyses | -is → -es |
| Basis | Bases | -is → -es |
| Crisis | Crises | -is → -es |
| Thesis | Theses | -is → -es |
| Phenomenon | Phenomena | Greek origin |
| Criterion | Criteria | Greek origin |
| Datum | Data | Latin origin |
| Appendix | Appendices | -ix → -ices |
| Index | Indices | -ex/-ix → -ices |
| Matrix | Matrices | -ix → -ices |
| Child | Children | Irregular |
| Person | People | Irregular |
| Man | Men | Irregular |
| Woman | Women | Irregular |
| Foot | Feet | Irregular |
| Tooth | Teeth | Irregular |
| Mouse | Mice | Irregular |
| Goose | Geese | Irregular |
| Ox | Oxen | Irregular |
| Leaf | Leaves | -f → -ves |
| Life | Lives | -f → -ves |
| Wolf | Wolves | -f → -ves |
| Shelf | Shelves | -f → -ves |
| Cactus | Cacti/Cactuses | Both accepted |
| Fungus | Fungi | -us → -i |
| Nucleus | Nuclei | -us → -i |
| Syllabus | Syllabi/Syllabuses | Both accepted |
| Formula | Formulae/Formulas | Both accepted |
| Medium | Media | Irregular |
| Bacterium | Bacteria | Irregular |

#### 9.3 Detector Table: Parts of Speech
| Word Type            | Typical Endings                                          | Example Words                                                                                  | Quick Listening Clues                                                               |
| :------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------- | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| *==Noun==*           | -tion, -ment, -ness, -ity, -ance, -ence, -ship, -er, -or | information, agreement, happiness, ability, attendance, difference, friendship, teacher, actor | Often after articles (a, the), quantifiers (some, many), or possessives (my, their) |
| *==Verb==*           | -ate, -en, -ify, -ise/-ize                               | activate, strengthen, clarify, organize                                                        | Often after subjects (he, they), modals (can, will), or to + verb                   |
| *==Adjective==*      | -able, -al, -ful, -ic, -ive, -less, -ous, -y             | comfortable, personal, helpful, scientific, creative, careless, famous, happy                  | Often before nouns, after 'be', or with 'very', 'quite'                             |
| *==Adverb==*         | -ly, -ward(s), -wise                                     | quickly, backwards, otherwise                                                                  | Often after verbs, before adjectives, or at sentence start                          |
| *==Auxiliary Verb==* | be, have, do, will, can, may, must, should, might        | is, are, was, have, had, do, does, will, can, must                                             | Used to form questions, negatives, or tenses                                        |
| *==Preposition==*    | (no fixed ending)                                        | in, on, at, by, with, about, under, between                                                    | Usually before nouns/pronouns, show relation                                        |
| *==Conjunction==*    | (no fixed ending)                                        | and, but, or, so, because, although, while                                                     | Link clauses or ideas                                                               |
| *==Interjection==*   | (no fixed ending)                                        | oh, wow, hey, ouch, alas, well                                                                 | Standalone, express emotion                                                         |

##### 9.3.1 Prediction Tip

- Listen for the word before/after the blank. If it's an article or quantifier, expect a noun. If it's a modal, expect a verb. If it's 'very', expect an adjective or adverb.

#### 9.4 Sentence Type Detector

| Sentence Type      | Structure                                                                                    | Key Audio Clues                                               | Example                                                           |
| :----------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------ | :---------------------------------------------------------------- |
| *Simple*           | One independent clause                                                                       | One subject, one verb, no connectors                          | "The train leaves at six."                                        |
| *Compound*         | Two independent clauses joined by coordinating conjunction (and, but, or, so, yet, for, nor) | Listen for 'and', 'but', 'so', etc.                           | "She studied hard, and she passed the test."                      |
| *Complex*          | One independent + one or more dependent clauses (because, although, since, when, if, etc.)   | Listen for 'because', 'although', 'since', 'when', 'if', etc. | "Although it was raining, they went outside."                     |
| *Compound-Complex* | Two or more independent + at least one dependent clause                                      | Multiple connectors, longer sentences                         | "She finished her work, and she went home because she was tired." |

Prediction Patterns
- If you hear a long pause or a change in intonation, expect a new clause or sentence type.
- Dependent clauses often start with 'if', 'when', 'because', 'although', 'since', 'unless', 'while', 'after', 'before', 'as soon as'.
- Coordinating conjunctions ('and', 'but', 'so') signal compound structure.

---

#### 10.1 Numbers, Dates, and Currency Traps

> [!tip] **Numbers and Dates are a Top Source of Lost Marks**
> IELTS loves to test your ability to catch numbers, years, and prices in fast, accented speech. These are often paraphrased or pronounced in unexpected ways.

##### 10.1.1 Numbers
- **Years:**
    - 2013 = "twenty thirteen" (not "two thousand and thirteen")
    - 1908 = "nineteen oh eight"
- **Decimals:**
    - 0.5 = "nought point five" (UK), "zero point five" (US)
- **Fractions:**
    - 1/2 = "a half"; 3/4 = "three quarters"
- **Large Numbers:**
    - 1,500 = "one thousand five hundred" or "fifteen hundred"
- **Phone Numbers:**
    - 0800 123 4567 = "oh eight hundred, one two three, four five six seven"
    - Double numbers: 33 = "double three"; 555 = "triple five"

##### 10.1.2 Dates
- **Written:** 12/06/2026 (UK: 12th June 2026, US: June 12th, 2026)
- **Spoken:** "the twelfth of June, twenty twenty-six" or "June twelfth, twenty twenty-six"
- **Traps:**
    - "the first of May" vs. "May first"
    - "in the nineteen eighties" = 1980s

##### 10.1.3 Currency
- **Pounds (£):** <mark style="background: #ABF7F7A6;">"pounds", "quid" (slang), "pence" (p), "a fiver" (£5), "a tenner" (£10)</mark>
- **Dollars ($):** <mark style="background: #ABF7F7A6;">"bucks" (slang), "cents"</mark>
- **Euro (€):** <mark style="background: #ABF7F7A6;">"euros", "cents"</mark>
- **Trap:** <mark style="background: #ABF7F7A6;">Listen for "about", "roughly", "just under", "over" before numbers</mark>


##### 10.1.4 More Band 9 Number/Date/Currency Traps & Practice

- **Ordinal Numbers:** 1st (first), 2nd (second), 3rd (third), 21st (twenty-first)
- **Years in Speech:** 2000 = "two thousand"; 2001 = "two thousand and one"; 2010 = "twenty ten"
- **Complex Dates:** "the twenty-second of February, nineteen ninety-nine"; "February twenty-second, nineteen ninety-nine"
- **Currency in Context:**
    - "It cost just under fifty quid." (means: slightly less than £50)
    - "The ticket was about a tenner." (means: about £10)
    - "It’s five bucks a piece." (means: $5 each)
- **Practice Drill:**
    - Listen to BBC news or podcasts and write down every number, date, and price you hear. Check for UK/US differences.

---

#### 10.2 Collocations & Idiomatic Language

> [!info] **Band 9 Audio Uses Natural Collocations and Idioms**
> These are phrases native speakers use together. They often change meaning in context and are a Band 9 marker.

##### 10.2.1 High-Frequency Collocations

| Base Word   | Common Collocations                                                                                                           |
| :---------- | :---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Make        | make a decision, make progress, make an effort, make a difference, make a mistake, make a suggestion, make money, make a call |
| Take        | take a risk, take a break, take responsibility, take part, take notes, take a seat, take action, take advantage               |
| Do          | do homework, do research, do a favor, do business, do the dishes, do your best, do harm, do exercise                          |
| Get         | get ready, get lost, get the impression, get involved, get permission, get married, get rid of, get the chance                |
| Pay         | pay attention, pay a visit, pay the price, pay respect, pay a compliment, pay a bill, pay interest                            |
| Break       | break the news, break a record, break the ice, break a habit, break the law, break even, break down                           |
| Keep        | keep in touch, keep a promise, keep an eye on, keep a secret, keep calm, keep the change, keep up the good work               |
| Education   | pass an exam, attend a lecture, submit an assignment, take a course                                                           |
| Work        | meet a deadline, earn a living, climb the career ladder, work overtime                                                        |
| Health      | catch a cold, break a leg, suffer an injury, make a recovery                                                                  |
| Travel      | catch a train, miss a flight, book a ticket, pack a suitcase                                                                  |
| Environment | reduce pollution, conserve energy, recycle waste, protect wildlife                                                            |


##### 10.2.2 Idioms & Phrasal Verbs

| Idiom/Phrasal Verb             | Meaning                               | Example                                             |
| :----------------------------- | :------------------------------------ | :-------------------------------------------------- |
| ==**Hit the books**==          | ==Start studying seriously==          | ==I need to hit the books for my exam.==            |
| ==**Piece of cake**==          | ==Very easy==                         | ==The test was a piece of cake.==                   |
| **Under the weather**          | Feeling ill                           | She’s under the weather today.                      |
| **Call it a day**              | Stop working for now                  | Let’s call it a day and go home.                    |
| **Get the hang of**            | Learn how to do something             | You’ll get the hang of it soon.                     |
| **Run out of**                 | Have no more left                     | We ran out of milk.                                 |
| **Look forward to**            | Anticipate with pleasure              | I look forward to meeting you.                      |
| **Take after**                 | Resemble a family member              | She takes after her mother.                         |
| **Let the cat out of the bag** | Reveal a secret                       | He let the cat out of the bag about the party.      |
| **Once in a blue moon**        | Very rarely                           | We go out for dinner once in a blue moon.           |
| **The ball is in your court**  | It’s your decision/responsibility     | The ball is in your court now.                      |
| **Burn the midnight oil**      | Work late into the night              | She burned the midnight oil to finish her essay.    |
| **On the same page**           | In agreement                          | We’re on the same page about the plan.              |
| **Pull someone’s leg**         | Joke or tease                         | Are you pulling my leg?                             |
| **Back to the drawing board**  | Start again from the beginning        | The plan failed, so it’s back to the drawing board. |
| **Break the bank**             | Cost too much                         | That car will break the bank.                       |
| **Bring up**                   | Mention                               | She brought up an interesting point.                |
| **Carry on**                   | Continue                              | Please carry on with your story.                    |
| **Give up**                    | Stop trying                           | Don’t give up!                                      |
| **Look into**                  | Investigate                           | I’ll look into the problem.                         |
| **Put off**                    | Postpone                              | They put off the meeting.                           |
| **Turn up**                    | Arrive                                | He turned up late.                                  |
| **Come across**                | Find by chance                        | I came across an old photo.                         |
| **Get along with**             | Have a good relationship              | I get along with my classmates.                     |
| **Look after**                 | Take care of                          | She looks after her brother.                        |
| **Make up**                    | Invent (a story)                      | He made up an excuse.                               |
| **Set up**                     | Arrange, establish                    | They set up a new company.                          |
| **Take off**                   | Become successful or leave the ground | The business took off quickly.                      |
| **Work out**                   | Solve, exercise                       | We worked out the solution.                         |
| **Turn down**                  | Refuse                                | She turned down the offer.                          |
| **Go over**                    | Review                                | Let’s go over the answers.                          |
| **Hand in**                    | Submit                                | Please hand in your homework.                       |
| **Look up to**                 | Admire                                | I look up to my teacher.                            |
| **Put up with**                | Tolerate                              | I can’t put up with the noise.                      |
| **Run into**                   | Meet by chance                        | I ran into an old friend.                           |
| **Take up**                    | Start (a hobby)                       | I took up painting.                                 |
| **Break down**                 | Stop working (machine) or get upset   | The car broke down.                                 |
| **Bring about**                | Cause                                 | The new law brought about changes.                  |
| **Come up with**               | Think of (an idea)                    | She came up with a great idea.                      |
| **Drop out**                   | Leave (school, course)                | He dropped out of college.                          |
| **Get by**                     | Manage, survive                       | I can get by with little money.                     |
| **Give in**                    | Surrender                             | He gave in to the pressure.                         |
| **Go on**                      | Continue                              | Please go on.                                       |
| **Hold on**                    | Wait                                  | Hold on a second.                                   |
| **Look out**                   | Be careful                            | Look out for cars!                                  |
| **Pass out**                   | Faint                                 | He passed out from the heat.                        |
| **Pick up**                    | Learn, collect                        | She picked up Spanish quickly.                      |
| **Put on**                     | Wear                                  | Put on your jacket.                                 |
| **Set off**                    | Start a journey                       | We set off early in the morning.                    |
| **Show up**                    | Arrive                                | He didn’t show up to class.                         |
| **Take care of**               | Look after                            | I’ll take care of it.                               |
| **Turn on/off**                | Start/stop a device                   | Turn on the lights, please.                         |
| **Work out**                   | Exercise, solve                       | I work out at the gym.                              |
| **Hit the books**              | Start studying seriously              |                                                     |
| **Piece of cake**              | Very easy                             |                                                     |
| **Under the weather**          | Feeling ill                           |                                                     |
| **Call it a day**              | Stop working for now                  |                                                     |
| **Get the hang of**            | Learn how to do something             |                                                     |
| **Run out of**                 | Have no more left                     |                                                     |
| **Look forward to**            | Anticipate with pleasure              |                                                     |
| **Take after**                 | Resemble a family member              |                                                     |
- "bring up" (mention), "carry on" (continue), "give up" (stop trying), "look into" (investigate), "put off" (postpone), "turn up" (arrive)




---

#### 10.3 Listening for Attitude, Opinion, and Inference

> [!question] **Band 9 Questions Often Test Implied Meaning**
> You must catch not just facts, but the speaker’s attitude, opinion, or what is suggested but not directly stated.

##### 10.3.1 Attitude/Opinion Markers

- "Frankly", "To be honest", "I’m afraid", "Fortunately", "Unfortunately", "Honestly", "In my view", "If you ask me", "I’m convinced", "I doubt that", "I’m not sure", "I have no doubt"
- "I suppose", "I guess", "I’d say", "It seems that", "I reckon", "I suspect", "I wonder", "I’m certain"
- "Apparently", "Supposedly", "Allegedly", "Evidently", "Presumably", "Seemingly"

##### 10.3.2 Tone and Attitude in Listening

- Sarcastic: "Oh, that’s just great…" (means the opposite)
- Enthusiastic: "That’s fantastic!", "I can’t wait!"
- Hesitant: "Well, I’m not sure…", "I suppose so…"
- Disappointed: "That’s a shame.", "I was hoping for better."

##### 10.3.3 Inference Triggers

- "He didn’t say it directly, but…"
- "From what she said, we can infer…"
- "The tone was sarcastic/enthusiastic/uncertain."
- "It’s not stated, but it’s clear that…"
- "Reading between the lines…"
- "The implication is…"


---

#### 10.4 Time Management & Transfer Skills

> [!tip] **Many Band 9 Candidates Lose Marks During the Transfer Stage**
> The last 10 minutes are for transferring answers. This is a high-risk time for errors.

##### 10.4.1 Time Management

- Use the reading time before each section to preview questions, not to check previous answers.
- During the test, move on quickly if you miss an answer—don’t freeze.
- In the last minute, scan for any blanks and fill them with your best guess.




#### 11.1 Alphanumeric Codes & Numbers

Section 1 always tests your ability to write down complex strings of letters and numbers (postcodes, flight numbers, phone numbers). You cannot afford to drop a single point here.

**The "Double" and "Triple" Rule:**
- If a phone number is 077888, the British speaker will say: "Oh, double seven, triple eight."
- The number 0 is almost always pronounced as "Oh" in UK English phone numbers and room numbers, not "Zero".

**Phonetic Alphabet Traps (Letters that sound the same):**
- **A vs. E vs. I:** (A = /eɪ/, E = /iː/, I = /aɪ/)
- **J vs. G:** (J = /dʒeɪ/, G = /dʒiː/)
- **M vs. N:** Wait for the speaker to clarify (e.g., "N for November, M for Mike").

**Dates and Formats:**
- Audio: "The 3rd of September."
- Write: 3 SEPTEMBER or 3RD SEPTEMBER or 3 SEPT. (Do not write 03/09 as date formats confuse UK/US markers; stick to the word).

**Currencies & Measurements:**
- You do not need to write the symbol if it is already printed on the question paper (e.g., if the paper says £ _____, just write 50).
- If the symbol is missing, write the word or symbol: 50 POUNDS or £50.

#### 11.2 Fractions, Decimals, and Percentages

Part 3 and 4 lectures often involve statistical data. You must know how native speakers dictate numbers.

**Fractions:**
- 1/3 → "A third" or "One third".
- 1/4 → "A quarter" or "One quarter" (NOT "one fourth").
- 3/4 → "Three quarters".
- 1/2 → "A half".

**Decimals:**
- 0.5 → "Nought point five" or "Zero point five" or simply "Point five".
- 1.25 → "One point two five" (Never "One point twenty-five").

#### 11.3 Connected Speech (Native Phonological Traps)

When native British speakers talk at normal speed, words crash into each other. If you are listening for clear, robotic separation between words, you will miss the answer.

**Elision (Dropping sounds):**
- When a word ends in 't' or 'd' and the next word starts with a consonant, the 't/d' is often dropped.
- You hear: "Nex door" → You write: NEXT DOOR
- You hear: "Las week" → You write: LAST WEEK

**Assimilation (Blending sounds):**
- 'd' + 'y' = 'j' sound (e.g., "Would you" sounds like "Wood-joo").
- 't' + 'y' = 'ch' sound (e.g., "Don't you" sounds like "Doan-choo").
- 'n' followed by 'b' or 'p' sounds like 'm' (e.g., "Ten pens" sounds like "Tem pens").

**Weak Forms (The Schwa /ə/):**
- Prepositions and articles are rarely stressed.
- "A piece of cake" sounds like "A piece uh cake". Listen for the stressed nouns and verbs; don't get lost trying to hear the weak prepositions.

#### 11.4 Compound Nouns: One Word or Two?
The word count instruction ("Write NO MORE THAN ONE WORD") makes compound nouns a minefield. You must know which words are merged and which are separated.

| Compound Noun         | Form         | Example/Notes                                 |
| :------------------- | :----------- | :-------------------------------------------- |
| Airport              | One word     | Arrive at the airport                         |
| Background           | One word     | Background noise                              |
| Blackboard           | One word     | Write on the blackboard                       |
| Bookstore            | One word     | Go to the bookstore                           |
| Breakdown            | One word     | Car breakdown                                 |
| Classroom            | One word     | In the classroom                              |
| Database             | One word     | Computer database                             |
| Feedback             | One word     | Give feedback                                 |
| Footprint            | One word     | Carbon footprint                              |
| Greenhouse           | One word     | Greenhouse gases                              |
| Handwriting          | One word     | Neat handwriting                              |
| Homework             | One word     | Do your homework                              |
| Keyboard             | One word     | Computer keyboard                             |
| Laptop               | One word     | Use a laptop                                  |
| Newspaper            | One word     | Read the newspaper                            |
| Notebook             | One word     | Take notes in your notebook                   |
| Overhead             | One word     | Overhead projector                            |
| Password             | One word     | Enter your password                           |
| Playground           | One word     | Children in the playground                    |
| Rainfall             | One word     | Heavy rainfall                                |
| Seafood              | One word     | Eat seafood                                   |
| Textbook             | One word     | Buy a textbook                                |
| Toothpaste           | One word     | Use toothpaste                                |
| Workshop             | One word     | Attend a workshop                             |
| Wildlife             | One word     | Protect wildlife                              |
| Website              | One word     | Visit a website                               |
| Deadline             | One word     | Meet the deadline                             |
| Waterproof           | One word     | Waterproof jacket                             |
| Underground          | One word     | Underground station                           |
| Upload               | One word     | Upload a file                                 |
| Download             | One word     | Download the document                         |
| Classroom            | One word     | Quiet classroom                               |
| Password             | One word     | Secure password                               |
| Workshop             | One word     | Science workshop                              |
| Greenhouse           | One word     | Greenhouse effect                             |
| Footprint            | One word     | Environmental footprint                       |
**Always ONE word:**
- Wildlife
- Seafood
- Background
- Waterproof
- Textbook
- Notebook
- Newspaper
- Workshop
- Greenhouse
- Footprint

**Always TWO words:**
- Post office
- High school
- Living room
- Ice cream
- Full time / Part time (Unless hyphenated as an adjective: A FULL-TIME JOB)

**Hyphenated (Counts as ONE word):**
- Up-to-date
- State-of-the-art
- Self-esteem
- Mother-in-law
- Check-in
#### 11.5 Error-routing map

- If losses come from spelling/capitalization: [[Common_Misspelled_Words_IELTS|Common Misspelled Words]] and [[Spelling_Rules_for_IELTS|Spelling Rules for IELTS]].
- If losses come from grammar form (singular/plural): [[Singular_vs_Plural_and_Pronoun_Agreement|Singular vs Plural and Pronoun Agreement]] and [[Subject_Verb_Agreement|Subject Verb Agreement]].
- If losses come from lexical paraphrase mismatch: [[Paraphrasing_Strategies|Paraphrasing Strategies]] and [[Collocations]].
- If losses come from attention collapse or trap misread: [[Common_English_Mistakes|Common English Mistakes]] and [[Grammar_Accuracy_QA|Grammar Accuracy QA]].
